message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>BACKGROUND</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Currently, therapeutic options for ambulatory COVID-19 patients are limited.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>OBJECTIVE</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To evaluate the safety, efficacy and effect of the intramuscular administration of polymerized type I collagen (PTIC) on hyperinflammation, oxygen saturation and symptom improvement in adult outpatients with symptomatic COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>DESIGN</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled clinical trial of PTIC vs placebo.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>SETTING</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Single Third-level hospital in Mexico City (Instituto Nacional de Ciencias Médicas y Nutrición Salvador Zubirán)</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>PARTICIPANTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Eighty-nine adult participants with a confirmed COVID-19 diagnosis and symptom onset within the 7 days preceding recruitment were included from August 31, 2020 to November 7, 2020 and followed for 12 weeks. Final date of follow-up was February 4, 2021.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>INTERVENTIONS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Patients were randomly assigned to receive either 1.5 ml of PTIC intramuscularly every 12 h for 3 days and then every 24 h for 4 days (n=45), or matching placebo (n=44).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The primary outcome was a mean reduction of at least 50% in the level of IP-10 compared to baseline. The secondary outcomes were mean oxygen saturation ≥92% while breathing ambient air and duration of symptoms.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>RESULTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Of 89 patients who were randomised, 87 (97.8%) were included in an intention-to-treat analysis; 37 (41.6%) were male and mean age was 48.5±14.0 years. The IP-10 levels decreased 75% in the PTIC group and 40% in the placebo group vs baseline. The comparison between treatment vs placebo was also statistically significant (
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.0047). The IL-8 (44%,
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.045), M-CSF (25%,
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.041) and IL-1Ra (36%,
                    <jats:italic>P=</jats:italic>
                    0.05) levels were also decreased in the PTIC group vs baseline. Mean oxygen saturation ≥92% was achieved by 40/44 (90%), 41/42 (98%) and 40/40 (100%) of participants that received PTIC at 8, 15 and 97 days of follow-up vs 29/43 (67%), 31/39 (80%) and 33/37 (89%) of patients treated with placebo (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.001). The unadjusted accelerated failure time model showed that patients treated with PTIC achieved the primary outcome 2.70-fold faster (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.0001) than placebo. In terms of risk, the group of patients treated with PTIC had a 63% lower risk of having a mean oxygen saturation <92% vs placebo (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.0001). Symptom duration in patients treated with PTIC was reduced by 6.1±3.2 days vs placebo. No differences in adverse effects were observed between the groups at 8, 15 and 97 days of follow-up.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>CONCLUSIONS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this study, treatment with PTIC down-regulated IP-10, IL-8, M-CSF and IL-Ra levels, which could explain the PTIC effect on the higher proportion of patients with mean oxygen saturation readings ≥92% and a shorter duration of symptoms as compared to patients treated with placebo. Although results are encouraging, larger randomised trials are needed.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>TRIAL REGISTRATION</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="http://ClinicalTrials.gov">ClinicalTrials.gov</jats:ext-link>
                    Identifier:
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="clintrialgov" xlink:href="NCT04517162">NCT04517162</jats:ext-link>
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Effect of polymerized type I collagen in hyperinflammation of adult outpatients with symptomatic COVID-19: a double blind, randomised, placebo-controlled clinical trial
